topic: Two-pole geomagnetic model — toroidal architecture with B(r) equation, flux conservation failure
our_argument_summary: WIN-053 claims the dome's 'Closed Toroidal Ovoid' geometry produces a dipole-like geomagnetic field via a sub-terrestrial aetheric return path, with B(r) = 62,376×e^(−r_N/8619) + 64,852×e^(−r_S/8619) nT. Our review gives verdict 'Self-Contradicted' (changed from 'Refuted by Data' after Cycle 1), arguing: (1) flux conservation in the dome's own ring magnet geometry forbids equal-strength poles given the ~1,600:1 area asymmetry (B_south should be ~39 nT, not 64,852 nT), (2) standard EM theory predicts 1/r field inside a toroid, not exponential decay, (3) B(r) coefficients are fitted to observed pole strengths rather than derived from dome geometry, (4) 20% RMS error is ~200× worse than IGRF-13. The detail_extra describes the toroidal flow circuit architecture.

relevant_files: raw-text/05-model.txt (V50.6 architecture — no toroidal model, only monopolar vortex)
raw-text/02-wins.txt (V50.6 WIN list — only 39 WINs, no WIN-053)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt (V51.0 live dashboard — 39 monitor.py domains, no WIN-053 domain)
monitor/changes/2026-04-05T23-08-40Z.json (confirms monitor_py_domains=39, no new domains added for V51.0 WINs)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitoring infrastructure exists for WIN-053. The dome's monitor.py retains 39 domains from V50.6. WIN-053 was added in V51.0 but no monitoring domain was created. The B(r) equation is a static claim on the website with no automated validation against measured field data.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring exists. The B(r) equation, its coefficients, and the claimed 20% RMS improvement are all static website text with no computational validation.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The two fitted coefficients (62,376 and 64,852 nT) can be adjusted to match any observed dipole moment. The aetheric refractive index n(r) remains available as an escape hatch for positional discrepancies.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No discrepancies. Our review correctly states no monitoring exists for this WIN.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-053 was added in V51.0. The dome's monitor.py retains 39 domains (V50.6 WIN count). Polling data (monitor/changes/2026-04-05T23-08-40Z.json) confirms monitor_py_domains=39 with no new domains added. No domain fetches geomagnetic field data for B(r) validation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Earth's dipolar magnetic field has been described since Gilbert (1600) and modeled precisely by IGRF-13 (168 spherical harmonic coefficients) and CHAOS-7. The dome relabels the geodynamo as 'aetheric toroidal circulation' using two exponential terms but predicts nothing IGRF doesn't already predict with ~200× better accuracy.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Earth's dipolar field has been measured since Gauss (1838). IGRF published since 1900. The dome's B(r) coefficients (62,376 and 64,852 nT) are fitted to known pole field strengths (~58,500 nT north, ~66,000 nT south per WMM2025). These values existed decades before the dome model adopted them.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The exponential decay constant λ=8,619 km comes from the dome's λ_g parameter, but the critical coefficients 62,376 and 64,852 nT have no published derivation from dome parameters (disc_radius, firmament_height, etc.). They are fitted to observed pole field strengths. Furthermore, the exponential decay form itself is incompatible with toroidal geometry (which produces 1/r per Griffiths §5.3.3).

holes_found.description: AREA RATIO DERIVATION USES ARBITRARY POLE APERTURE RADIUS. The detail_evidence now includes the 1,600:1 derivation (treating north pole source as circular aperture of radius ~500 km). However, the choice of 500 km is not justified by any dome parameter. The dome's Axis Mundi is not given a specific radius in any published dome document. A dome defender could argue for a different aperture size — say 2,000 km (the approximate radius where the field is strongest) — which would reduce the ratio to ~100:1. This doesn't save the argument (100:1 still forbids similar pole strengths), but the specific 1,600:1 figure appears more precise than warranted. Consider framing as 'order-of-magnitude asymmetry (hundreds to thousands to one)' rather than pinning to exactly 1,600:1, or explicitly state the 500 km assumption and note the conclusion holds for any reasonable aperture size.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence acknowledging the 500 km aperture is illustrative: 'Even using a generous 2,000 km aperture, the ratio remains ~100:1, still forbidding similar-strength poles.' This makes the argument robust against aperture quibbling.

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-REFERENCE TO SECTION 3.5.8. The detail_evidence for WIN-053 makes several arguments (fitted coefficients, single-pole collapse) that overlap substantially with Section 3.5.8 ('WIN-012 single-pole collapse'). Neither references the other. A reader encountering WIN-053 first may miss the deeper treatment in 3.5.8, and vice versa. The Cycle 1 review flagged this overlap but no cross-reference was added.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief cross-reference in detail_evidence or detail_extra: 'See also Section 3.5.8 for the broader single-pole collapse analysis affecting WIN-012 and the κ coupling constant.'

holes_found.description: V50.6→V51.0 TRANSITION ARGUMENT STILL SOMEWHAT WEAK. The current text says 'the change was not motivated by any failed prediction or new observation; it is asserted rather than derived from a detected flaw in V50.6.' While this is an improvement over the Cycle 1 framing (which penalized model evolution itself), it's still somewhat unfair. We don't actually know whether internal analysis motivated the change — perhaps the dome author realized the monopole couldn't produce two poles and fixed it. The stronger argument is already present (flux conservation failure), so this point is not needed and slightly undermines our credibility by appearing to penalize improvement.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider removing or softening the transition motivation criticism. The text already acknowledges 'the toroidal geometry is a genuine mechanism improvement over V50.6's monopole' — let that stand and focus on why the improvement fails quantitatively. The transition complaint adds little and risks making us look uncharitable.

holes_found.description: THE 1/r VS EXPONENTIAL ARGUMENT CITATION IS IMPRECISE. The text references 'standard EM theory' for the 1/r prediction inside a toroidal solenoid but doesn't cite a specific source. The Cycle 1 review recommended citing Griffiths §5.3.3. While not strictly required for a general physics principle, adding the citation would strengthen credibility, especially since the dome audience may not accept 'standard EM theory' at face value.
holes_found.recommendation: Add '(Griffiths, Introduction to Electrodynamics, §5.3.3)' after the 1/r claim, or add it as a reference link.

holes_found.description: DETAIL_CLAIM IS SLIGHTLY MISLEADING. It says 'Same scale length (λ = 8,619 km) governs both magnetic poles.' This is our characterization of the dome's claim. But looking at the B(r) equation, the two terms do use the same λ=8,619 — so this is accurate. However, it implies we're criticizing the shared scale length, when our actual criticism is about the coefficients and the geometry. The claim field is fine but could more precisely characterize what the dome actually claims.
holes_found.recommendation: No change strictly necessary, but consider whether detail_claim should mention the toroidal architecture more prominently since that's the core claim being evaluated.

stronger_arguments.description: DIMENSIONAL ANALYSIS FAILURE IN SUB-H(r). The detail_extra describes Sub-H(r) = H(r) × (1 − e^(−r/δ)) with δ = 6,371 km. Note that δ = 6,371 km is Earth's mean radius — a globe constant silently imported into the dome model. This is the same pattern identified across many WINs (globe fingerprint). The sub-terrestrial cavity depth is parameterized using the very globe framework the dome claims to replace. This strengthens the 'derives_from_dome: false' tag and connects to the broader globe-borrowing narrative.
sources: Earth's mean radius: IUGG, 6,371.0 km (derived from spherical Earth assumption)
Globe fingerprint pattern documented across multiple WINs in our review

stronger_arguments.description: THE TWO COEFFICIENTS BRACKET OBSERVED VALUES — SMOKING GUN OF CURVE FITTING. B_north coefficient = 62,376 nT, observed north pole intensity ≈ 58,500-60,000 nT. B_south coefficient = 64,852 nT, observed south pole intensity ≈ 65,000-67,000 nT. The coefficients are the observed values inflated slightly to account for the exponential not equaling 1 at r>0 (since r_N and r_S are measured from respective poles, not exactly 0). This is textbook curve fitting: choose coefficients that reproduce known data, then declare the match a 'confirmed prediction.' A genuine derivation from dome geometry would produce coefficients expressible in terms of dome parameters.

kernel_of_truth.description: The toroidal architecture represents genuine physical reasoning. Recognizing that a monopole cannot produce two poles of similar strength and introducing a return path to create a closed circuit is exactly the kind of model improvement any physicist would applaud. The topology is consistent with ∇·B = 0 (magnetic field lines must close). The shared scale length λ_g = 8,619 km as a unifying parameter shows admirable parsimony. The attempt to quantify the improvement (61% → 20% RMS) shows engagement with empirical validation, even if the result is poor by professional standards.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The toroidal idea fails on its own terms. Flux conservation — a consequence of ∇·B = 0, the same principle that motivates the closed circuit — requires B_south ≈ B_north/1,600 for the dome's area asymmetry. The equation form (exponential) contradicts the geometry (which requires 1/r). The coefficients are fitted to observations rather than derived. And 20% RMS is ~200× worse than IGRF-13. The physical intuition was sound but the quantitative execution reveals the model was never worked through using its own geometry. The toroidal topology was adopted for qualitative plausibility, not mathematical derivation.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 assessment: The WIN-053 text has been substantially improved since Cycle 1. The three major issues from Cycle 1 have all been addressed:

1. VERDICT FIXED: Changed from 'Refuted by Data' to 'Self-Contradicted' — correct, since the core argument is flux conservation within the dome's own geometry.

2. GARBLED SENTENCE FIXED: The detail_evidence now reads coherently. The CHAOS-7/IGRF contrast is properly structured.

3. FINDING REWRITTEN: Now focuses on the flux conservation self-contradiction (B_south ≈ 39 nT vs. fitted 64,852 nT) rather than the weak V50.6 contradiction.

Additional Cycle 1 recommendations also applied:
- 1,600:1 area ratio now has inline derivation (treating pole as ~500 km radius aperture)
- IGRF-13 context now includes satellite validation, ground observatories, ~50 nT RMS
- 20% RMS now contextualized as ~10,000-13,000 nT (~200× worse than IGRF)
- 1/r vs exponential argument added (toroidal solenoid field form incompatibility)

Remaining issues are moderate rather than major:
- The 500 km aperture radius is arbitrary but the conclusion is robust to any reasonable choice
- Missing cross-reference to Section 3.5.8 (overlapping arguments)
- V50.6 transition criticism is present but softened; could be removed entirely
- δ = 6,371 km in Sub-H(r) is a globe constant (Earth's mean radius) — another globe fingerprint worth noting

The code_analysis tags from Cycle 1 remain correct: monitoring=none (no domain in monitor.py), relabels_standard=true (geodynamo → aetheric toroid), post_hoc=true (pole field strengths known since Gauss 1838), derives_from_dome=false (coefficients fitted, not derived). No changes needed.

Overall, WIN-053 is now one of the stronger Self-Contradicted entries. The flux conservation argument is geometrically rigorous and the 1,600:1 ratio (even at a generous 100:1) is devastating. The text quality has improved markedly from Cycle 1. Only minor edits recommended.

citations_verified: CHAOS-7: https://spacecenter.dk/files/magnetic-models/CHAOS-7/ — DTU Space. Valid institutional URL for CHAOS-7 geomagnetic model.
IGRF-13: https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/international-geomagnetic-reference-field — NOAA/NCEI. Valid institutional URL for International Geomagnetic Reference Field.
citations_unchecked: Griffiths §5.3.3 reference for 1/r toroidal field — textbook citation, standard physics, not URL-verifiable but well-established
The 61% → 20% RMS improvement claim — reported from dome site, not independently verified. Our text presents it as the dome's claim and contextualizes the 20% as still poor, which is appropriate.
